summary: This is an update slide about Ovaria IPT, a project from AthenaDAO focused on menopause prediction modeling. Gero AI developed machine learning models to predict age at natural menopause (ANM) using UK Biobank data, correcting for self-reporting biases. The work includes GWAS and Mendelian Randomization analysis linking menopause timing to disease risk.

content_blocks.title: Menopause Risk Model (MRM) - Model Development
content_blocks.content: Gero AI developed models to predict age at natural menopause (ANM) in UK Biobank women. The models correct for self-reporting biases and recall distortion ('telescoping') in ANM data. Covariates included in modeling: BMI, smoking, and deprivation index. Two ML approaches tested: (1) Gradient-boosted trees - achieved highest accuracy, (2) Accelerated failure Weibull model - similar performance but far more interpretable.

content_blocks.title: GWAS and Mendelian Randomization Analysis
content_blocks.content: Using the developed models, Gero performed Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS) and Mendelian Randomization to establish links between menopause timing, genetics, and disease risk. This represents a computational/bioinformatics approach rather than wet-lab experimentation.

content_blocks.title: Key Findings - Disease Correlations
content_blocks.content: Earlier menopause shows observational correlation with >100 diseases. Potential true causal effects identified with osteoporosis and endometriosis specifically. Target validation: findings replicated across various datasets, suggesting robustness of the associations.

content_blocks.title: Document Metadata
content_blocks.content: Project: Ovaria IPT from AthenaDAO. Partner/Collaborator: Gero AI (appears to be the modeling/analysis team). Social media call-to-action suggests this is a community update or early-stage project announcement. Contact: athenadao.co, community@athenadao.co, @athena_dao_

key_findings: Machine learning model developed for menopause age prediction using UK Biobank data - computational approach with gradient-boosted trees and Weibull models
GWAS and Mendelian Randomization analysis completed linking menopause timing to disease risk - suggests TRL 1-2 (computational target identification)
No wet-lab experimental validation shown - purely computational/bioinformatics analysis
Disease associations identified: >100 observational correlations, potential causal links to osteoporosis and endometriosis
Target replication across multiple datasets provides some validation of computational findings
No information on: therapeutic modality, drug candidates, cell/animal models, efficacy data, IP status, regulatory strategy, or development timeline
Appears to be early discovery stage - computational target identification without experimental validation
